title: Steamed Fish with Ginger and Spring Onion

ingredients.name: whole fish or thick fillets (pomfret, seabass, snapper or tilapia)
visual_checks: a whole fish gives the best flavour; scored on both sides for even cooking
ingredients.notes: a whole fish gives the best flavour; scored on both sides for even cooking

subs.name: firm tofu or thick slices of paneer
subs.notes: steam tofu for 6 minutes; the soy-ginger sauce works beautifully

ingredients.name: ginger, half sliced and half julienned
spring onions, half in 5 cm stalks and half julienned

subs.name: a light salt brine + a tiny splash of vinegar
subs.notes: soy sauce is central to this dish — seek it out

subs.name: skip the sesame oil drop and add a little extra soy sauce

ingredients.name: neutral cooking oil
visual_checks: heated until smoking and poured over the fish at the end
ingredients.notes: heated until smoking and poured over the fish at the end

steps.text: Clean and pat the fish dry. If using a whole fish, score 3 diagonal slashes on each side down to the bone. Rub the fish lightly with salt and place the stalks of spring onion and sliced ginger underneath the fish on a heat-proof plate that fits inside your steamer or pot.

Set up a steamer or wok-steamer rig: place a round rack or heatproof bowl in the bottom of a wide pot. Add 5 cm of boiling water and bring to a rolling boil. Place the plate of fish on the rack, cover tightly with a lid and steam on high heat for 8–10 minutes for a whole fish (6–7 minutes for fillets). The flesh should be just opaque and flake easily at the thickest point.
Mix the soy sauce, sesame oil, sugar and 2 tablespoons of water in a small bowl. The sauce should taste pleasantly savoury and slightly sweet.
When the fish is done, carefully pour off any water that has collected on the plate. Discard the cooked spring onion and ginger from underneath.
Arrange the julienned fresh ginger, spring onion and green chilli in a pile on top of the fish. Pour the soy-sesame sauce over the fish.

Heat the neutral oil in a small saucepan until it just begins to smoke — wisps of smoke are your signal. Immediately and carefully pour the smoking-hot oil over the ginger and spring onion mound on the fish. The oil will sizzle loudly, releasing the fragrance of the fresh aromatics. Serve at once.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
Follow the cooker manufacturer's fill, venting and opening instructions; never force the lid open under pressure.
critical_checks: Cook seafood until safely done for the species and preparation; keep it chilled before cooking.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: fish → firm tofu or thick slices of paneer (steam tofu for 6 minutes; the soy-ginger sauce works beautifully); soy-sauce → a light salt brine + a tiny splash of vinegar (soy sauce is central to this dish — seek it out); gingelly-oil → skip the sesame oil drop and add a little extra soy sauce
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Pomfret (paplet), tilapia and seer fish (vanjaram) all steam beautifully. If you do not have a proper steamer, place a metal rack or upturned steel bowl inside a large pot with a well-fitting lid — it works perfectly. The pressure cooker works too: put 1 cup of water in the cooker, place the plate of fish on a raised stand inside, and steam on low heat (without the weight) for 8 minutes. Gingelly oil (nallennai) is your sesame oil. Soy sauce is essential here and widely available.
